content.problemStatement: Chef recently learned about ratios and proportions. He wrote some positive integers **a, b, c, d** on a paper. Chef wants to know whether he can shuffle these numbers so as to make some proportion? Formally, four numbers **x, y, z, w** are said to make a proportion if ratio of **x : y** is same as that of **z : w**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated positive integers a, b, c, d (1 ≤ a, b, c, d ≤ 1000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing "Possible" if the four numbers can be reordered to form a proportion x:y = z:w, otherwise output "Impossible".

sampleTestCases.explanation: By reordering to 1 2 2 4 we get 1:2 = 2:4, i.e., 1*4 = 2*2, so a proportion exists.
No ordering of the four numbers yields equal cross products; therefore a proportion cannot be formed.

solution.approach: A proportion x:y = z:w holds iff x*w = y*z. We need to know whether any ordering of the four given numbers satisfies this equality. There are only 4! = 24 possible permutations, so we can simply try all of them. For each permutation (x, y, z, w) we check if x*w == y*z. If any permutation works we answer "Possible", otherwise "Impossible". The algorithm runs in O(24) per test case, i.e., O(T) overall, and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work (24 checks) per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
